attributes.stem:
A 66-year-old man is admitted to the rehabilitation ward two weeks after a pontine stroke. He has a medical history of hypertension and hypercholesterolaemia. His medications include aspirin, perindopril, and atorvastatin.

On examination, he appears alert. His vital signs are within normal limits. He demonstrates preserved consciousness and cognitive function but has complete paralysis of all voluntary muscles except for vertical eye movements and blinking. He is unable to speak or swallow and is receiving nutrition through a nasogastric tube. He has secretion and cough management protocols in place with regular suctioning. His family reports they are uncertain whether he can understand them.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate first priority for rehabilitation?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with classic features of locked-in syndrome, characterised by quadriplegia and anarthria with preserved consciousness and vertical eye movements. The condition typically results from a pontine lesion affecting the corticospinal and corticobulbar tracts while sparing the reticular activating system.

Establishing communication system is the highest priority in locked-in syndrome rehabilitation. This typically begins with establishing a reliable yes/no communication system using eye movements or blinking. Once this is achieved, more sophisticated augmentative and alternative communication (AAC) devices can be introduced. This is crucial for assessing cognition, determining needs and preferences, and enabling participation in further rehabilitation decisions. Without communication, it would be impossible to assess the patient's comfort, pain levels, or respiratory distress, making all other interventions potentially unsafe or ineffective. Establishing communication also allows the clinical team to explain procedures, obtain consent, and involve the patient in their care plan, which is essential for patient-centred rehabilitation.

Respiratory muscle training is indeed critical in locked-in syndrome due to the risk of respiratory complications, including ineffective cough and secretion management. However, even though the patient already has basic secretion and cough management in place, implementing more advanced respiratory interventions requires a way to communicate with the patient about comfort and effectiveness. Without established communication, the patient cannot signal respiratory distress or the need for intervention, making respiratory management less effective and potentially distressing.

Prevention of contractures through passive range of motion exercises is important but should not take precedence over establishing communication. The ability to communicate discomfort during passive movements is essential for safe and effective physical therapy, and for the patient to indicate when stretching causes pain or when positioning is uncomfortable.

Swallowing rehabilitation is a longer-term goal. While dysphagia management is crucial, the immediate priority is to establish a way for the patient to communicate basic needs and participate in rehabilitation planning. Swallowing assessment and therapy would be more effective once communication is established, as the patient can provide feedback on sensations and difficulties.

Early mobilisation program is a key goal of post-stroke care, but would be difficult to implement effectively without first establishing a way to communicate with the patient about comfort, pain, and preferences. Mobilisation without communication could lead to undetected pain or discomfort, potentially causing psychological distress and reducing engagement with rehabilitation.
learningPoints: Early augmentative and alternative communication is the highest priority in locked-in syndrome rehabilitation to enable basic interaction and assess needs.
Locked-in syndrome is characterised by quadriplegia and anarthria with preserved consciousness and vertical eye movements.
A systematic approach to establishing reliable yes/no communication is essential before proceeding with other rehabilitation goals including respiratory management.
references: Smith E, Delargy M. Locked-in syndrome. BMJ. 2005;330(7488):406-409. doi:10.1136/bmj.330.7488.406. Available online at ; https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC549115/
Farr E, Altonji K, Harvey RL. Locked‐in syndrome: practical rehabilitation management. PM&R. 2021 Dec;13(12):1418-28. Available online at ; https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1002/pmrj.12555
